behaviors.description: Speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so that the summary can attribute statements to specific attendees.
behaviors.justification: A mock STT API can always return well-formed speaker labels, passing unit-level tests while hiding the real failure: format incompatibility between the actual STT API's output and what the downstream LLM expects. The real boundary is whether the actual API's label format (field names, nesting, confidence scores, speaker ID schemes) survives our pipeline untouched. We need a realistic or recorded STT response to catch mismatches in how we parse/forward labels.
behaviors.test_description: Load a realistic speech-to-text response fixture (from real API or replay) with speaker labels in native format, verify the service preserves all label metadata (speaker IDs, timestamps, confidence) and forwards it to the LLM prompt in the same structure without dropping or reordering fields.

behaviors.description: The structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who was assigned a task during the meeting.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends entirely on what the real LLM produces, not on our code logic. A mock LLM can be trivially configured to always include action items for every attendee, making all lower-level tests pass while hiding the real failure: the LLM sometimes forgets, misses, or misattributes action items to the wrong person. Only a real LLM call can reveal whether it reliably enforces this constraint across varied meeting types and speaker patterns.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM with a transcript where multiple attendees are explicitly assigned distinct, unambiguous tasks (e.g., 'Alice will handle the design review', 'Bob will write the test plan'). Verify the structured output contains at least one action item for Alice and one for Bob, correctly attributed by name.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the 'decisions' section of the summary, not buried in the narrative.
behaviors.justification: Like behavior 2, this is a constraint on LLM output structure. A mock LLM can be configured to always populate the decisions field correctly, but the real LLM may fail to identify what counts as a 'key decision', may conflate decisions with action items, or may place them in the narrative instead. Only a real LLM call reveals whether it consistently applies the decision extraction rule.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM with a transcript containing 2-3 clear key decisions (e.g., 'We decided to postpone launch to Q2', 'We're switching vendors for the API'). Verify each appears in the structured 'decisions' section with minimal or no duplication in the narrative summary text.

behaviors.description: When the audio file is longer than 60 minutes, the service splits it into segments and processes them sequentially, merging the results into a single coherent summary.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is not code logic but real audio file handling and external STT API behavior. Unit tests with mocked STT can verify merge logic, but cannot catch: where the audio is actually segmented (mid-word vs sentence boundary), how speaker labels persist across segment boundaries, whether the real STT API's output format changes when given partial audio. A mock always returns predictable transcripts; real audio and real (or deeply realistic) STT responses expose edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Process a real or recorded audio file longer than 60 minutes (or a realistic 70+ minute synthetic file). Verify the service automatically splits it, calls the STT API multiple times (once per segment), preserves speaker continuity across boundaries, and produces a merged transcript where speaker turns flow naturally without abrupt breaks or lost context.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output — completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording.
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end pipeline test requiring orchestration of multiple real steps: real audio file upload, real STT API calls (with segmentation/merging from behavior 4), real LLM call for summarization, and structured output delivery. Any single step can fail in production (auth errors, rate limits, API schema changes, network timeouts) and must be handled gracefully. Unit/Integration tests can cover individual steps; only a Workflow test running the full real pipeline catches integration points and error handling across all steps.
behaviors.test_description: Upload a real meeting recording (or realistic synthetic audio file). Let it process through the complete, uninterrupted pipeline: audio ingestion → STT transcription with automatic segmentation if needed → LLM summarization → structured JSON output with transcript, summary, action items, decisions, and speaker attributions. Verify the final output is complete and valid (no missing fields, no partial transcripts) without any user prompts or retries.
